old_claim.note: Databases (Relational DBMS) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Expert systems encode knowledge bases and inference rules in software; relational DBMS is not a prerequisite for early systems such as DENDRAL.

source_shape.source_locator: Dendral overview identifying Dendral as an early expert system used for chemical analysis.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The overview frames Dendral as an expert-system milestone, which supports anchoring the node to rule-based AI software rather than relational database systems.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This partially supports the software edge because expert systems are software artifacts with encoded rules; it rejects relational DBMS as a prerequisite by chronology and scope.

ontology_before: Symbolic expert systems depended on relational DBMS, making 1960s expert systems downstream of a 1970 database model.
ontology_after: Symbolic expert systems depend on AI, formal logic, and software engineering; relational DBMS is not a hard lineage edge.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: symbolic_ai_expert_systems no longer directly depends on databases_relational_dbms and now depends on software_engineering.
would_reject_if: A source showed early expert systems required relational DBMS.
The graph reintroduced symbolic_ai_expert_systems to databases_relational_dbms.
The node were re-scoped specifically to database-backed commercial expert-system shells.
short_reason: Early expert systems are rule-based software, not relational database systems.
